coredata.dc:title: Highly enhanced photoluminescence and suppressed blinking of N-doped carbon dots by targeted passivation of amine group for mechanistic insights and Vis-NIR excitation bioimaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Yatao
Qiao, Lulu
Zhu, Zhichao
Ran, Xia
Kuang, Yanmin
Chi, Zhen
He, Yulu
Liu, Renming
Guo, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention owing to their attractive optical properties, small size, low cost, and good biocompatibility. However, there are still challenges in unequivocally unraveling the photoluminescence (PL) origin and the structure-function relationship associated with the surface groups of CDs. Herein, we have successfully enhanced the PL efficiency of GA/N-CDs by targeted passivation of the amine groups of N-doped CDs (N-CDs) with glutaraldehyde (GA), to facilitate their bioimaging applications under one-photon and two-photon excitations. In particular, we have systematically investigated the regulation effects of this surface modification on the optical properties, exciton dynamics, and blinking behavior using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopies, and single-particle fluorescence imaging microscopes. The effective passivation of the amine surface group of N-CDs not only regulates the energy levels and properties of surface states, but importantly activates an additional emissive center via an aldehyde-amine condensation reaction. Meanwhile, the hydrogen bonding interactions between GA and the related surface groups of N-CDs further restrain the non-radiative exciton relaxations. This synergetic effect greatly enhances the PL efficiency of two separate emission centers and the photostability of GA/N-CDs. Moreover, the photoblinking of N-CDs is significantly suppressed and the two-photon excitation PL property is also markedly increased after the surface modification, endowing GA/N-CDs the outstanding performances for fluorescence bioimaging under Vis-NIR excitations. Our findings contribute to mechanistic insights into the correlation between photophysical properties and surface states/groups, as well as demonstrate an effective post-modification strategy to improve the PL efficiency and stability of carbon-based dots.

head.ce:title: Highly enhanced photoluminescence and suppressed blinking of N-doped carbon dots by targeted passivation of amine group for mechanistic insights and Vis-NIR excitation bioimaging application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention owing to their attractive optical properties, small size, low cost, and good biocompatibility. However, there are still challenges in unequivocally unraveling the photoluminescence (PL) origin and the structure-function relationship associated with the surface groups of CDs. Herein, we have successfully enhanced the PL efficiency of GA/N-CDs by targeted passivation of the amine groups of N-doped CDs (N-CDs) with glutaraldehyde (GA), to facilitate their bioimaging applications under one-photon and two-photon excitations. In particular, we have systematically investigated the regulation effects of this surface modification on the optical properties, exciton dynamics, and blinking behavior using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopies, and single-particle fluorescence imaging microscopes. The effective passivation of the amine surface group of N-CDs not only regulates the energy levels and properties of surface states, but importantly activates an additional emissive center via an aldehyde-amine condensation reaction. Meanwhile, the hydrogen bonding interactions between GA and the related surface groups of N-CDs further restrain the non-radiative exciton relaxations. This synergetic effect greatly enhances the PL efficiency of two separate emission centers and the photostability of GA/N-CDs. Moreover, the photoblinking of N-CDs is significantly suppressed and the two-photon excitation PL property is also markedly increased after the surface modification, endowing GA/N-CDs the outstanding performances for fluorescence bioimaging under Vis-NIR excitations. Our findings contribute to mechanistic insights into the correlation between photophysical properties and surface states/groups, as well as demonstrate an effective post-modification strategy to improve the PL efficiency and stability of carbon-based dots.

Surface modification to enhance emission and suppress blinking of N-CDs for Vis-NIR excitation fluorescence imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention owing to their attractive optical properties, small size, low cost, good biocompatibility, and simple synthetic routes from diverse natural substances [1–4]. The color-tunable photoluminescence (PL), excellent photostability, nontoxicity, and easy functionalization have endowed CDs as promising candidates for various applications such as bioimaging, biomedicine, photocatalysis, display and optoelectronics [5–8]. Generally, CDs are considered to possess carbonized cores (either sp2 hybrid crystalline form or sp3 hybrid amorphous carbon structure) with functional surface groups including carbonyls, carboxylic acids, hydroxyls, epoxides, amines, and so on [9–12]. The optical properties of CDs are easily influenced by the used precursors and synthetic methods [13,14], thus rendering CDs with the advantage of facile surface modifications. In the past decade, numerous efforts have been made to improve the photoluminescence property and explore the application fields of CDs materials [15–17]. However, the exact mechanism remains a conundrum, and a convincing structure-property relationship of fluorescent CDs is not established due to the disparate emission characteristics resulting from different synthetic methods, precursors, and post-treatments. Without the understanding of PL origin, it would be difficult to effectively synthesize CDs with desired brightness at working wavelength and design appropriate photoelectronic devices for practical applications. Recently, the primary viewpoints for the PL origin of CDs have been proposed as carbon core state, surface state, molecule state and their synergistic effect [18–21]. Due to the complexity of structure and mechanism, excitation dependent/independent fluorescence, multiple band emission, and different PL spectral regions with different quantum yields (QY) of CDs can be found in the literature [10,22,23]. Excitation-dependent and multiple bands PL reflect there are separate emission centers from different excited states mentioned above. A specific CD in the literature usually has its corresponding mechanism because of the different structures, types, and content of excited states, and different optical properties. It is still difficult to discriminate the contribution of different states and formulate a convincing mechanism even for certain types of excited states.

The surface states of CDs, usually dominating the n–π* electron transitions in lower-energy regions, are considered to control the electronic structures and optical properties by hybridizing carbon backbone and connecting surface functional groups. The energetic bandgaps of surface states, PL properties and exciton recombination dynamics can be significantly adjusted by altering the types and contents of surface functional groups. Besides heteroatom doping, the post-treatments of CDs by surface passivation and functionalization have demonstrated effectiveness in regulating the photophysical properties of CDs, such as shifting PL wavelength, increasing radiative recombination, improving the photostability and biocompatibility in aqueous solution [10]. These also offer the opportunity for deep insight into the mechanisms and structure-function relationship of emissive CDs by modifying the surface states. For example, Qu's group demonstrated that blue-emissive CDs treated with aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were regulated to green-emissive CDs with a PL quantum yield of 25% [24]. The bathochromic-shifted emission was ascribed to a new exciton recombination channel formed by oxidized surface states. Sun's group reported the multiple-color-emission CDs by controlled graphitization and surface function groups [25]. The emission wavelength can be tuned from 430 to 630 nm by controlling the extent of graphitization and the amount of –COOH functional groups. Liu et al. reported that the energy gap of CDs reflects the coupling between the carbon core and carbonyl groups, leading to different fluorescence QY [26]. Kundelev et al. conducted a theoretical study on the role of surface amino groups as optical centers to regulate the absorption and fluorescence properties of CDs, and they found that amino groups can induce spectral red-shifting and enhance fluorescence intensity [27]. Nguyen and coworkers employed scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) to directly image individual solvothermal-synthesized CDs with a sub-particle resolution and investigate the fluorescence mechanism [28]. They suggested that oxygen-rich surface groups are principally responsible for the fluorescence origin of CDs and more oxidized surface defects lead to a red-shift emission. To date, it is increasingly adopted that the surface states associated with the functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups) play a significant role in improving the electronic structure, single and multiple photon properties, and photostability of CDs. However, the challenge remains to separate the contribution of different functional groups to the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs. Similar to the one-photon property, the two-photon excitation (TPE) PL and photostability of CDs are also closely associated with the surface functional groups, which is highly desirable for biomedical fields due to its low cell damage, large penetration depth and efficient resistance of autofluorescence [29–31]. Comparatively, the multiple-photon properties and the blinking behavior of CDs with surface modification are few reported and worthy of further exploration. Therefore, a comprehensive knowledge of how surface groups with different characteristics affect the PL behavior is essential for understanding the structure-function relationship of CDs and engineering CDs with high quantum yield, good photostability for single or multiple photon excitation bioimaging.

In this work, we have successfully modified the surface states, electronic structure, PL properties and dynamics of CDs through the surface reaction between primary amines of N-CDs and aldehydes of glutaraldehyde (GA), together with hydrogen bonding between N–H/O–H and C=O groups. The synthesis process of GA/N-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The introduction of GA onto the N-CDs surface dramatically regulates the energetic levels of original surface states, effectively increases the one-photon absorption cross sections and blue-shifts the PL spectrum, particularly activating the non-radiative surface states of the amine group to highly emissive states. The increased radiative recombination of the two emission centers significantly enhance PL with QY changing from 7% for N-CDs to 38% for GA/N-CDs in an aqueous solution and 56% in DMF solvent. Meanwhile, the GA modification greatly suppresses the PL blinking behavior and improves the photostability of GA/N-CDs. Moreover, the two-photon properties of as-prepared GA/N-CDs have also been enhanced to facilitate their PL nucleolus imaging of living cells under NIR excitation. These findings are significant for deeply understanding the contribution of different surface functional groups to the optical properties of CDs, and provide a targeted strategy of surface modification for improving the emission performance of CDs as TPE imaging probes.

N-CDs were prepared according to our reported procedure [32]. Briefly, 0.5 g o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was dissolved in a 20 mL aqueous solution under ultrasound conditions. Then, the solution was heated at 200 °C for 12 h to form N-CDs in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the solution containing N-CDs was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove the impurities. The obtained solution was filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed in a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 12 h. For the preparation of GA/N-CDs, an appropriate amount of GA aqueous solution was added to the as-prepared N-CDs. After stirring for 10 h at room temperature, the GA/N-CDs were purified using the same purification protocol as that for N-CDs. The crude GA/N-CDs product was filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed by a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 12 h to remove the unreacted GA molecules, during which the dialysate water was changed every 4 h. The final GA/N-CDs powder was obtained through a freeze-drying process at −60 °C and 15 Pa for 24 h.

For the Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) measurements, a drop of prepared CDs solution was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid followed by overnight air-drying. TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were recorded on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 Transmission Electron Microscope. For Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurements, CDs solution (10 μL) was deposited onto the freshly cleaved mica. After incubation for 2 min, the mica substrate was washed with ultra-pure water and dried with nitrogen gas. The morphologies of CDs were characterized with a commercial AFM (Slover P47H, NT-MDT) in tapping mode. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was carried out using Al/Kα radiation as the monochromatized X-ray source at the operating power of 300 W (ThermoFisher Nexsa). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a PerkinElmer spectrometer (Spectrum One B). The CDs powder was pressed into a tablet with KBr for FT-IR measurement. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CDs powders were recorded on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. The scan range between 20° and 60° was set for a continuous scan at a speed of 0.6°/min.

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were taken with a Cary 5000 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent Inc.) and PerkinElmer LS-55 PL spectrophotometer, respectively. Time-resolved PL (TRPL) measurements were performed using the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) method (Harp300, Picoquant). A broadband tunable pulse femtosecond Ti:sapphire laser (Chameleon Ultra II, Coherent Inc.) was used as the excitation source with a pulse width of around 120 fs. The 410 nm fs excitation pulse was generated from a BBO nonlinear optical crystal, with a changeable repetition rate changing from 80 MHz to 10 MHz as required. The emission signal passed through a monochromator and was detected by a micro photon device (MPD, Picoquant). The instrumental response function (IRF) was less than 50 ps. Femtosecond transient absorption (TA) measurements were performed using a regenerative amplified Ti:sapphire laser system (Astrella Vitara-S, Coherent Inc.) and a Helios Fire Femtosecond TA Spectrometer (Ultrafast Systems). Briefly, the 800 nm output pulse from the regenerative amplifier was split into two parts with a beam splitter. The reflected part was used to pump an optical parametric amplifier (TOPAS, Coherent Inc.) and generate the tunable wavelength from 290 nm to 2600 nm as the pump/excitation beam. A small portion of the transmitted part was focused on a CaF2 plate to produce a supercontinuum from 320 nm to 750 nm as the probe beam. The sample was placed in a 2.0 mm cuvette with magnetic stirring. The IRF of TA systems is around 140 fs. The dynamic traces obtained from TRPL and TA were fitted with the following multi-exponential function convoluted with their corresponding IRF,

Two-photon absorption (TPA) measurements of CDs were carried out using the femtosecond TOPAS as the laser source. A circular lens with a focal length of 100 mm was utilized to excite the sample contained in a 2-mm-thick quartz cuvette with magnetic stirring. The PL signals under different excitation wavelengths and power were collected and detected by an imaging spectrograph coupled with an EMCCD(SR-163, ANDOR). The TPA cross-section of CDs was determined by fitting the correlation between excitation power and PL intensity, using a relative method with RhB as the standard.

The single particle samples for PL blinking measurements were prepared by spin-casting CDs dilute solution in aqueous of nanomole concentration onto a clean coverslip and then covered by poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) of 0.5 wt% to avoid oxidization from oxygen in the air. The Hanbury Brown and Twiss (HBT) system was utilized to measure the second-order photon correlation function with time g2(τ) and prove the single-particle fluorescence imaging of CDs [33,34]. The excitation power at 458 nm from broadband femtosecond laser was set to 40 nW with a repetition rate of 8 MHz. The emission from the fluorescence lifetime imaging microscope (FLIM with a 100 × oil objective, Olympas,) passed through a long-pass filter (AT495lp, Chroma) and a 50/50 beam-splitter, and then was detected by two single-photon detectors (MPD, PicoQuant). Time-dependent photon correlation function g2(τ) was recorded and analyzed with a commercial Sympho-Time64 software (PicoQuant). The value of g2(0) lower than 0.5 is the criterion to prove the single-photon emission from a single particle [35]. Single-particle blinking measurements were performed on a total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM, Ti-E 2000, Nikon) equipped with a 100 × oil immersion objective (N.A. = 1.49), and a CW 458 nm laser (Coherent, USA) was used as the excitation source. To make sure the single-particle imaging and single photon emission for the situation of TIRFM, we estimated the applied excitation power by referring to the power density of g2(τ) measurement on FLIM. Taking the pulse excitation of 100 nW as an example, the power density should correspond to that of 50 mW of CW laser excitation. Therefore, single-photon emission of CDs can be realized as long as the CW excitation power is lower than 50 mW for single-particle TIRFM experiments. All the PL blinking data were collected from single-particle TIRFM under the CW excitation power of lower than 4.0 mW. The PL signals of single CDs went through a dichroic mirror (ZT458rdc, Chroma) and a long-pass filter (AT465lp, Chroma), and were recorded by an EMCCD (Evolve Delta, Photometrics) with a time resolution of 30 ms. The bin time was set to 100 ms for blinking analysis. A threshold to distinguish the “ON” and “OFF” events in blinking traces was determined by the following formula [36,37],
(2)

where I

An MTT assay was applied to evaluate the toxic effect of GA/N-CDs on HeLa cells. Briefly, HeLa cells were cultured with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) in 96-well plates for cell attachment. After the overnight incubation, GA/N-CDs or N-CDs solutions with various concentrations (0–200 μg/mL) were added into the wells and incubated for 24 h. Then, 100 μL MTT (0.5 mg/mL) was added followed by another 5 h of incubation. Finally, 150 μL of the formazan-dissolving solution was added and mixed by gently shaking. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured with Infinite M Plex (Tecan) to assess cell viability. For cell imaging, the HeLa cells grown on the pre-clean coverslips in 6-well plates at 37 °C were incubated with 10 μg/mL GA/N-CDs for 2.5 h. Subsequently, the collected cells were fixed with 1 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde and washed 3 times with PBS. PL images of HeLa cells incubated with GA/N-CDs were recorded on a single-photon/two-photon confocal laser scanning microscope (A1R MP+, Nikon) equipped with a broadband tunable Ti:sapphire laser (Chameleon II, Coherent Inc.) from 680 nm to 1080 nm. The one-photon (458 nm) excitation or TPE (from 750 nm to 1000 nm) PL imaging was carried out using a 25 × water immersion objective (N.A. = 1.10, W.D. = 2.0 mm) and gallium arsenide phosphide (GaAsP) non-descanned detectors (NDD).

The surface modification of N-CDs with GA is designed to realize through the chemical reaction between the amine group on N-CDs and the carbonyl group of GA, and the synergic interaction via hydrogen bonding. The characterization of N-CDs and GA/N-CDs was performed by several microscopic and spectroscopic techniques, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 in the Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI). The TEM image of GA/N-CDs (Fig. 1a) indicates a monodispersed quasi-spherical morphology with an average size of 3.0 ± 0.8 nm. The AFM image (Fig. 1b) shows the height profile ranging from 2.0 to 5.0 nm of GA/N-CDs with an average height of 3.5 nm (Fig. 1c and 1d), which is consistent with the TEM analysis. HR-TEM images reveal that GA/N-CDs have a well-resolved lattice plane with a spacing of 0.2 nm (inset of Fig. 1a), which can be assigned to the (101) plane of the graphitic carbon core. The size distribution of GA/N-CDs from dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements exhibits no aggregate formed with introducing GA (Fig. S2a). The small change in hydrodynamic diameter could be attributed to the thickness of GA. The crystalline structure of GA/N-CDs was further characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis. There are two intense diffraction bands centered at 28° and at 41° (Fig. S2b), which can be attributed to the (002) and (101) planes of graphite structure with different interlayer spacing [38,39]. These features of GA/N-CDs are similar to those of N-CDs (Fig. S1c and Fig. S2d), suggesting that the interactions between GA and N-CDs occur through the functional groups of two moieties with little influence on the CDs core.

FT-IR and XPS measurements were performed to characterize the surface functional groups of N-CDs with and without GA modification. The FT-IR spectra of GA/N-CDs (Fig. 1e) show that the aldehyde peak at ∼1700 cm−1 of GA (Fig. S3) is decreased with capping on the surface of N-CDs [40,41]. This dramatic attenuation of 1700 cm−1 signal is ascribed to the Schiff base reaction between the primary amine groups on the CDs surface and the aldehyde groups of GA, leading to the generation of imine bonds at 1664 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum. However, there were also non-bonded C=O (∼1700 cm−1) stretching vibration bands observed in GA/N-CDs. Compared to the spectrum of N-CDs, the emerged bands at 2948 cm−1 and 2882 cm−1 indicate the presence of –(CH2)n– (n > 3) in GA/N-CDs, confirming the reaction between GA and N-CDs. In addition, GA/N-CDs exhibit the N–H/O–H stretching vibration mode at 3424 cm−1 along with shoulder peaks at 3269 cm−1 and 3136 cm−1, and C=O stretching vibration band at 1632 cm−1, suggesting that the N–H/O–H groups could be associated with the C=O via hydrogen bonding [42,43]. The XPS was employed to characterize the surface groups of GA/N-CDs and N-CDs (Fig. 2 and Fig. S4). The full XPS spectrum of GA/N-CDs shows four typical peaks centered at 286.5 eV, 400.8 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of electrons from C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively [44]. Composition analysis further gives the relative elemental contents of C (76.5%), N (4.5%), and O (23%) for GA/N-CDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be decomposed into C=N (286.4 eV), C–C (285.1 eV), and C=C (283.9 eV) bonds [8]. Compared with that of N-CDs (Fig. S4), the new species at 286.4 eV attributed to C=N bonds confirm the amine-aldehyde condensation reaction in GA/N-CDs. The decomposed three bands at 398.1 eV, 399.8 eV and 401.2 eV in the N 1s spectrum are ascribed to the N–H, C–N=C and N–C/N–N bands in GA/N-CDs (Fig. 2c) [45–47]. We can notice that the C–N=C groups exist in the pristine N-CDs, but the N content of ∼10% is much smaller than the C content of ∼70%, and the overall C=N content in pristine N-CDs would be small. The GA modification greatly increases the C=N density by passivating the N–H groups of N-CDs. Meanwhile, the formed C=O and C–O bonds, corresponding to the binding energies of 531.4 eV and 532.2 eV, can be observed in O 1s spectrum as well (Fig. 2d). These observed changes in FT-IR and XPS spectra suggest the formation of imine bonds through the condensation reaction between amine group on N-CDs and aldehyde group of GA, and the existence of hydrogen bonding interaction between N–H/O–H and C=O groups for GA/N-CDs.

By introducing GA onto the surface of N-CDs, we expected to regulate the electronic levels of surface states and improve the optical properties of GA/N-CDs by modifying the contents of surface functional groups and passivating the non-emissive surface sites. To prove this, steady-state spectroscopy and time-resolved PL measurements were performed to demonstrate the regulating effect on photophysical properties and get deep insight into the PL mechanism. For pristine N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a dominant band around 410 nm, mainly originating from the n–π* conjugate transitions associated with C–N and C=O bonds (Fig. 3 a) [8,44]. Intriguingly, this absorption band is separated into several discrete subbands for GA/N-CDs after surface modification. With the GA concentration increasing from 5 μL to 500 μL, the cross sections of both blue-side and red-side absorption are gradually increased, reflecting the changed energy levels and their different contributions to the electronic structure and optical properties from the functional surface groups of GA/N-CDs. This surface regulation effect on electronic structure is demonstrated in the spectral evolution characteristics, including the observed red-shifting, the appearance of new bands around 370 nm and 460 nm, and the multiple absorption bands at 380 nm, 430 nm, 460 nm and 503 nm. The bandgap modulation is also presented in the spectral changes with reaction time, due to the different rates of condensation reaction and hydrogen bonding between different functional groups on N-CDs and GA (Fig. S5). It should be mentioned that surface defects and the dielectric confinement effect could also partially contribute to the observed absorption evolution. The absorption band around 380 nm is mainly associated with the transition of C=C and C–N bonds in the core and surface groups, and the band around 460 nm with the shoulder bands around 430 nm and 503 nm primarily reflect the n-π* transitions of C=O and C=N bonds in the surface functional groups [46]. Correspondingly, the emission properties of GA/N-CDs are also remarkably regulated, compared to those of N-CDs (Fig. 3b). Pristine N-CDs show a dominant PL band with a maximum around 575 nm and the PL QY of 7% in aqueous solution. In sharp contrast, GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA) demonstrate a greatly enhanced PL with a blue-shifted and broadened band around 520 nm, and a high QY of 38% in aqueous solution under 410 nm excitation. The PL QY of GA/N-CDs can reach a much higher value of 56% in DMF and 54% in methanol (Fig. S6 and Table S1). Both N-CDs and GA/N-CDs demonstrate the independent excitation PL (Fig. 3c and Fig. S7). Specifically, the PL spectrum of GA/N-CDs can be decomposed into two separate Gaussian bands from two emission centers, while the PL spectrum of N-CDs is close to a single Gaussian distribution (Fig. 3d and e and Fig. S8). The relative ratio in PL intensity of these two emission bands for GA/N-CDs is rationally changed with increasing the GA concentration. In general, the competition between the radiative and non-radiative surface states associated with different surface functional groups plays an important role in regulating the PL properties of CDs [4,6,48]. We can infer that the surface modification activates a non-emissive center to an emissive one in GA/N-CDs, which is consistent with the absorption spectral evolution. Due to the efficient Schiff-base reaction, the amount of amine group is significantly decreased for GA/N-CDs, greatly blocking the non-radiative surface state of N-CDs. In other words, the surface state associated with the formed imine bonds is optically activated and transferred to a separate emissive center, resulting in multi-band and enhanced PL of GA/N-CDs. Meanwhile, the hydrogen bonding interaction between other surface functional groups, e.g., N–H/O–H and C=O groups, also effectively restricts the non-radiative vibrational relaxation of both emissive surface states and improves PL efficiency. Similar PL spectral features for oxidized and reduced surface species were also previously reported for other CDs [49].

Fig. 3f shows the PL decay curves at 620 nm of GA/N-CDs with different GA concentrations under 410 nm excitation. The PL decay of N-CDs can be well fitted with a single exponential function and gives a PL lifetime of 1.1 ns independent of excitation and emission wavelength (Fig. S9), suggesting the dominant contribution from a primary emissive center although there is a very weak emission in the long wavelength region from another center [32]. With the surface modification, the multi-exponential feature is getting more obvious in the PL dynamics of GA/N-CDs. All PL decay curves of GA/N-CDs can be decomposed into two components with almost invariable lifetimes of τ1∼2.3 ns and τ2∼10 ns, arising from two individual emission centers (Table 1 ). The short-lived component τ1 is associated with the original emission center in the blue region, while the long-lived component τ2 originates from the activated emission center in the red region. With increasing the GA concentration from 1 μL to 200 μL, the τ2 proportion can be significantly increased from 12% to 86%, demonstrating that the radiative exciton recombination of the activated surface state increasingly dominates the PL decay (Fig. S8). The relative ratio in amplitude shows good consistency with the PL spectral evolution of GA/N-CDs by changing the contents of GA. Compared to the 1.1 ns lifetime of N-CDs, the slightly increased lifetime τ1 (∼2.3 ns) reflects the intrinsic feature of this emission center of GA/N-CDs. These dynamical evolutions from two emissive centers and their relative contribution to PL intensity are also presented in the PL decay at different detecting wavelengths. Fig. 3g gives the PL dynamics of GA/N-CDs (2 μL GA) at the emission wavelengths of 490, 520, 560, 590 and 620 nm under 410 nm excitation, demonstrating an identical feature that is shown in Fig. 3f. We can notice that the PL decay curves become slower and the lifetime increases with the detection wavelength changing from 490 nm to 620 nm, and the short-lived component is succeeded by the long-lived component until the saturated surface modification (200 μL GA). Similarly, bi-exponentially fitting parameters of decay curves at different detection wavelengths can be obtained and summarized in Table S2. It is not surprising to see that the relative ratio in the amplitude of these two components changes with the emission wavelength, because of their different contributions to PL intensity. Fig. 3h and 3i corroborate the correlation of observed features in PL intensity and lifetime at different emission wavelengths. Combining the evolutions in absorption and PL spectra, PL QY, and emission dynamics with surface modification, we can conclude that the formation of imine bonds and hydrogen bonding interaction significantly regulate the type and density of surface states, activate an additional emissive state, suppress the non-radiative relaxation, and thus effectively improve the optical properties of GA/N-CDs. The blue-shifting evolution in the PL spectra of GA/N-CDs could be attributed to the increased dielectric confinement effect after surface passivation.

To gain insight into the improved PL with surface modification, we further performed femtosecond transient absorption (TA) measurements for GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA) and N-CDs under 400 nm excitation (Fig. 4 ). The negative signal around 410 nm corresponds to ground state bleaching (GSB), the positive band from 450 nm to 520 nm mainly arises from the excited state absorption (ESA), and the broad negative bands in the spectral region longer than 525 nm could be attributed to the stimulated emission (SE). It is worth mentioning that the GSB, ESA and SE signal with different signs and amplitudes are usually mixed, leading to the complexity of analyzing the whole TA spectra and dynamics. Here, we will focus on the SE signals to extend the PL dynamics from TRPL measurements. As shown in Fig. 3a, a weak tail absorption in the region longer than 525 nm can be observed, located in the similar spectral scope of PL for both N-CDs and GA/N-CDs. Therefore, the negative TA signal in this region could be the mixture of GSB and SE of two emission centers for GA/N-CDs, while literature indicated that a strong ESA usually covers the entire spectral region of CDs [50,51]. The TA spectra of N-CDs and GA/N-CDs at different time delays are shown in Fig. 4a and 4b. After 2.0 ps, we can suppose the negative GSB and SE signals in this longer spectral region follow the identical recovery/decay features arising from the same excited states (Fig. 4c). Fig. 4d represents the TA kinetic traces at 620 nm for N-CDs, 530 nm and 620 nm concerning two emission centers for GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA). Their corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table S3. The transient traces of GA/N-CDs at 530 nm and 620 nm can be well-fitted with a rise component and three decay/recovery components. The ultrafast growth component τg around 1 ps can be assigned to the average carrier relaxation from higher vibrational or excited states to the two emissive states. The fast component with tens of picoseconds reflects the average non-radiative deactivation, and the two slow decay/recovery processes of around 1.7 ns and 9.0 ns originate from the radiative recombinations of the two emission centers [52,53]. Likewise, the relative ratios of two emissive components indicate their different contributions to the dynamics of two separate emissive centers of GA/N-CDs. For the case of N-CDs at 620 nm, similar dynamical characteristics are observed including a rise component τg, a fast non-radiative process with tens of picoseconds and a slow radiative process τ2 of exciton recombination, while the contribution of the long-lived component τ3 could be negligible. These dynamical features in deactivation rate and especially in the relative amplitude ratio of two emission centers demonstrate a good consistency with the aforementioned results from TRPL measurements and PL spectral analysis. The existence of non-radiative fast processes via surface defects or functional groups should be responsible for the PL efficiency of 38% for GA/N-CDs in solution.

PL bioimaging probes should have the properties of high QY, a low-duty cycle of emission “ON”-“OFF” switching, a large penetration depth, and NIR excitation to resist autofluorescence [54,55]. In general, most probes display emission fluctuation with time, which is known as blinking and interpreted as an intermittency transition from a radiative “ON” state to a non-radiative “OFF” state. The surface modification is expected to improve the blinking behavior and photostability of GA/N-CDs to facilitate their bioimaging applications.

Before single-particle imaging and blinking observations, we made sure that the sub-nanomole concentration of CDs is used to prepare the sample and that the excitation power is low enough to exclude the influence of the multiple photon processes. More details about the experiments and analysis strategy can be found in the Material and Methods section. To achieve this criterion, we conducted the measurements of g2(τ) function on a single-particle FLIM. Usually, a value of g2(0) smaller than 0.5 can suggest single photon emission from a single particle [56]. For GA/N-CDs, we can notice that the average value of g2(0) is around 0.4 for a single bright spot in the image (Fig. 5 a and 5b), suggesting single-particle imaging and single photon emission under the excitation power of 40 nW at 458 nm from a femtosecond laser source. Fig. 5c and 5d shows the representative PL intensity trajectories of single-particle N-CD and GA/N-CD, and their corresponding distributions of integral photon count under the same experimental conditions. A striking difference in their blinking behaviors can be observed. To distinguish the emission “ON” and “OFF” states, we set a threshold three times of standard deviation above average background [57]. The PL trajectory of N-CDs demonstrates a dramatical blinking feature, alternatively switching between the “ON” state and the “OFF” state (Fig. 5c). After statistical analysis, we found that the “OFF” time fraction in 1 min is over 70% for N-CDs, which is consistent with the aforementioned low PL QY and the dominant non-radiative deactivation associated with the surface states. Fig. 5e and 5f illustrate the duration distributions of “ON” and “OFF” events. The distribution histograms can be fitted by a power law function, P(t)=At
−m , where P(t) is the probability density of blinking events, t is the duration time, and m is the power-law coefficient. Linearly fitting these “log-log” plots of single-particle N-CDs give the m values of 1.16 and 0.88 for the “ON” and the “OFF” states, respectively. These blinking observations suggest that the emission origination of N-CDs could be similar to that of semiconductor QDs, and multiple excited states/centers are probably responsible for the stochastic fluorescence intermittency [57,58]. Intriguingly, this intermittent blinking is significantly suppressed for GA/N-CDs with the “ON” time fraction larger than 95%, and the non-blinking events with a high probability have been observed in our experiments under CW excitation (Fig. 5d). As mentioned previously, the surface states associated with the functional groups of CDs play an important role in regulating the radiative and non-radiative exciton deactivations. Surface modification of N-CDs with GA effectively prevents the formation of the non-radiative surface states related to amide groups of N-CDs, and thus significantly suppresses the blinking and improves the photostability of GA/N-CDs by activating an additional emissive center and passivating the amide groups. These blinking observations provide more information for a deep understanding of the correlation between PL properties and surface states of CDs.

In addition to the enhanced PL properties and photostability of GA/N-CDs, the TPE emission could be also improved by the GA modification to facilitate their bioimaging application. The TPE PL properties of N-CDs and GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA) in solution were investigated using a tunable femtosecond laser as the NIR excitation source. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, Figs. S10 and S11, PL intensity of both N-CDs and GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA) shows a dependence of excitation power at the representative wavelengths of 860 and 960 nm. Obviously, GA/N-CDs demonstrate a much higher PL intensity than N-CDs under the same experimental conditions. Quantitative analysis indicates a quadratic relationship between the integrated intensity and excitation power, confirming the enhanced two-photon property by GA modification (Fig. 6b). We calculated the TPA cross-section δ2 using Rhodamine B as the standard reference and obtained a value of 942 GM (1 GM = 10−50 cm4photon−1s) for GA/N-CDs at 860 nm, much larger than the 96 GM for N-CDs [59]. This obtained δ2 value of GA/N-CDs in an aqueous solution is consistent with that of other CDs for TPE fluorescence imaging. For example, Lu and coworkers prepared TPE green emissive CDs using the o-phenylenediamine molecule as raw material and reported a δ2 of 1174 GM at 800 nm [48]. Jiang's group presented a TPE red emissive N-CDs with the δ2 value of 1450 GM at 700 nm, using the hydrothermal method and p-aminoazobenzene as carbon resource with N-doping [60]. Wu and co-workers reported a relatively small δ2 of 31.5 GM at 960 nm for the CDs using thiourea and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, while this δ2 value was increased to 207.4 GM after reacting with miRNA [61]. These obtained diverse δ2 values of CDs reflect their different structures and surface states associated with the reaction routes, conditions, and raw materials [23].

Based on the multiple advantages of small size, high PL QY in an aqueous solution, nearly non-blinking, and TPE properties, we assessed the potential applications of GA/N-CDs for one-photon and TPE fluorescence cellular imaging. The cytocompatibility was first evaluated at different concentrations of GA/N-CDs (200 μL GA). As shown in Fig. S12, the testing HeLa cells can keep more than 85% viability after 24 h of co-incubation with a concentration lower than 50 μg/mL of GA/N-CDs, suggesting good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity although the toxicity at the high dosage of over 100 μg/mL of GA/N-CDs might be considered in cell imaging (Fig. S13). The intracellular uptake performance of N-CDs and GA/N-CDs (10 μg/mL) was measured with the co-incubated HeLa cells at 37 °C for 2.5 h with single-photon fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 6c and 6d, both N-CDs and GA/N-CDs can pierce into HeLa cells and emit green PL with laser excitation at 458 nm. Compared to N-CDs, GA/N-CDs demonstrate a strong green PL in cells under the excitations at 458 nm and a high quality of imaging under the excitations at 860 nm and 960 nm (Fig. 6e and 6f), suggesting a facile cellular uptake and a promising TPE imaging capability. Interestingly, the GA/N-CDs can pass the cell membrane and stain cytoplasm, subsequently move into the nucleus and then localize in the nucleolar region. This observed targeting localization of GA/N-CDs could be associated with a lot of ribosomes in the cytoplasm and nucleus of tumor cells, as well as the weakened surface potential after GA modification (Fig. S14). These observations suggest that the water-soluble GA/N-CDs with good single-photon and TPE fluorescence properties, and good photostability can be well utilized in cell imaging with a certain target-specificity under Vis and NIR excitations.

In summary, we have successfully modified the surface states of N-CDs through the reaction between primary amines on the N-CDs surface and the aldehydes GA, together with the hydrogen bonding interactions between the related N–H/O–H and C=O groups. The introduction of GA onto the N-CDs surface dramatically regulates the density and types of surface states, and thus the electronic structures and optical properties of GA/N-CDs. The formed imine bonds and hydrogen bonds play a dominant role in increasing the absorption cross sections and PL QY, and particularly activating an additional emissive surface state of GA/N-CDs. The steady-state and transient spectroscopy studies reveal the combined contributions from two emission centers to the improved photophysical properties and the regulation mechanism of surface modification. Moreover, the significantly suppressed photoblinking and increased TPA cross-section facilitate the cellular imaging application of GA/N-CDs under visible-NIR excitations. Our findings provide important information for the mechanistic insight into the PL origin associated with the surface states/groups of CDs, as well as demonstrate a feasible strategy to improve the optical property and stability of carbon-based bioimaging probes.
